focus: Hallucination detection — does the baseline agent give incorrect DSQL guidance that the skill corrects?

evals.name: jsonb-column-hallucination
evals.expected_output: Confirms JSONB is supported as a stored type. States GIN indexes are not available — use btree on extracted keys or accept unindexed runtime queries. Does NOT recommend keeping the GIN index.
expectations: Confirms JSONB is a valid stored column type in DSQL (does NOT say to change it to JSON or TEXT)
States that GIN indexes are not available in DSQL — use btree on extracted columns instead
Does NOT recommend keeping the GIN index as-is or suggest it will work
Suggests extracting specific JSON keys to columns for indexed lookups, OR accepting unindexed @> queries
Uses CREATE INDEX ASYNC (not synchronous CREATE INDEX) in any index recommendation

evals.name: collate-hallucination
evals.prompt:
I'm migrating this PostgreSQL table to DSQL. Should I add COLLATE "C" to the string columns?

CREATE TABLE products (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  name VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  description TEXT,
  sku VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE
);
evals.expected_output: States that per-column COLLATE clause is NOT supported in DSQL (returns error). DSQL uses C collation database-wide automatically. Does NOT recommend adding COLLATE "C" to columns.
expectations: States that per-column COLLATE clause is NOT supported or will cause an error in DSQL
Explains that DSQL uses C collation database-wide (automatically, no per-column annotation needed)
Does NOT recommend adding COLLATE "C" to the column definitions
Mentions that ORDER BY behavior uses byte-value sorting (uppercase before lowercase) as a consequence of C collation
The DDL output does NOT include COLLATE "C" on any column

evals.name: sync-index-hallucination
evals.expected_output: Corrects the synchronous CREATE INDEX to CREATE INDEX ASYNC. Does NOT leave the indexes as synchronous CREATE INDEX.
expectations: Changes CREATE INDEX to CREATE INDEX ASYNC for all indexes
Does NOT leave any synchronous CREATE INDEX statement unchanged
Keeps tenant_id as the first column in composite indexes
Issues each DDL in its own transaction (or mentions this requirement)
